quoteFirst: The men now renewed their efforts with such alacrity and spirit, that before
quoteLast: in the morning the leak no longer gained upon them, but the pumps gained considerably upon the leak. They cut most of their cables with the consequent loss of anchors, but got once more under sail and stood for the land.
title: Pioneers in Australasia
author: Johnston, Harry, 1858-1927; Ball, Alec [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: While in Yedo we attended a meeting of missionaries, English, Scotch, and American, in a distant part of the city, and in the evening paid a visit to Prof. Verbeck, who has been here so long that he is an authority on all Japanese matters. It was
quoteLast: when we set out to return to our friends in the Foreign Office, and we bade our men take us through the main streets, that we might have a view of Yedo by night. The distance was some three miles, the greater part through the principal street.
title: From Egypt to Japan
author: Field, Henry M. (Henry Martyn), 1822-1907

quoteFirst: He was a majestic man, with a hooked nose, a keen dark eye, very large whiskers, and notions of his own as to how a boy--or his boy, at any rate--should be brought up.  First and foremost, he was to be a "gentleman"; which seemed to mean, chiefly, that he was always to wear a muffler and gloves, and be sent to bed, after a supper of bread and milk,
quoteLast: .  School-life, on experiment, seemed hostile to these observances, and Eugene was taken home again, to be moulded into urbanity beneath the parental eye.  A tutor was provided for him, and a single select companion was prescribed.
title: Eugene Pickering
author: James, Henry, 1843-1916

quoteFirst: Rations were cooked and the most thorough preparations made for the expected work of the morrow. Sunrise saw the old First in line, ready for the move.
quoteLast: came; no move, Nine--Ten, and yet no move. Arms had been stacked, and the men lounged lazily about the stacks.
title:
Red-Tape and Pigeon-Hole Generals
As Seen From the Ranks During a Campaign in the Army of the Potomac
author: Morford, Henry, 1823-1881; Armstrong, William H., 1824-1919; Frick, Jacob G.

quoteFirst: We set out from Porto Longone that night
quoteLast: , and next evening, driving through much-abated storm southward into calm waters and clear skies, reached Naples. At noon, Monte Circeo where Circe led her disreputable life, was a majestic rock against blue heaven and broken clouds; after nightfall, and under the risen moon, Vesuvius crept softly up from the sea, and stood a graceful steep, with wreaths of lightest cloud upon its crest, and the city lamps circling far round its bay.
title: Italian Journeys
author: Howells, William Dean, 1837-1920

quoteFirst: But in truth the artillery was employed merely in the vain hope of intimidating the assailants, or in order to cover the flight of the garrison. The Japanese encountered no resistance whatever.
quoteLast: in the morning they entered Chiu-lien. The enemy had decamped in the direction of Feng-hwang before dawn, leaving behind him almost everything, twenty-two guns, three hundred tents, large stores of ammunition and quantities of grain and forage.
title: The War in the East: Japan, China, and Corea
author: White, Trumbull, 1868-1941; Fireman, J. C. [Illustrator]; Morimoto, Teitoku [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: Morning seemed to come quickly. The boys were astir early, and had breakfast over by
quoteLast: . Then they crept out to the mouth of the tunnel, and were disappointed to find that the weather was unfavorable for departure. A fine rain was falling from a sodden, gray sky, and the air was quite warm and moist.
title: The Camp in the Snow; Or, Besieged by Danger
author: Graydon, William Murray, 1864-1946

quoteFirst: Most difficult times of day to get taxis are between 5 and 6, at the close of business hours;
quoteLast: at the theatre rush; 11 P.M., when the plays let out; and 4 A.M., when the night clubs close.
title: New York: Confidential!
author: Lait, Jack, 1882-1954; Mortimer, Lee, 1904-1963

quoteFirst: 'Sat up late! Why, you haven't been in bed at all. The bed isn't tossed. It's
quoteLast: . What made you sit up last night? Why, there's your colour all gone!'
title: Under St Paul's: A Romance
author: Dowling, Richard, 1846-1898

quoteFirst: All things being in a readiness, about
quoteLast: his Majesty came in his caroche attended with Prince Henry and the principal Lords of his Majesty's Council. The Lord Northampton met him before he came to the ordinary gate of the yard, and used all the means he could to have led his Majesty through Lydiard's garden by a back way into his house; but his Majesty told his Lordship that the Lord Admiral, whom he espied waiting with his train at the ordinary gate of the yard, would justly take exception at his so doing, for that it belonged properly there to his Lordship to receive and entertain
title: The Autobiography of Phineas Pett
author: Pett, Phineas, 1570-1647; Perrin, William Gordon, 1874- [Editor]
